questions.prompt: I can calculate derivatives, but what is the number actually telling me at a point: slope, accumulated area, or something else?

answers.text: On the graph, it is the slope of the tangent line at that point, assuming the derivative exists there.

feedback.legit: Geometrically it is the slope of the tangent line. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Geometrically it is the slope of the tangent line. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: I think of it as the function's instant rate of change, like zooming in until the local behavior looks linear.

feedback.legit: The instantaneous rate of change when the derivative exists. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: The instantaneous rate of change when the derivative exists. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Accumulated signed area belongs to definite integration, not to the derivative at one point.

feedback.legit: Definite integration, not differentiation, describes accumulated signed area. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Definite integration, not differentiation, describes accumulated signed area. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: Sanity check on the power rule: if `f(x) = x^n` for a positive integer `n`, what should `f'(x)` be?

answers.text: Bring the exponent down as a coefficient and reduce it by one: `f'(x) = n x^(n-1)`.

feedback.legit: `n x^(n-1)`. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: `n x^(n-1)`. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: You raise the exponent and divide, so it becomes `x^(n+1)/(n+1)`. Same move as the power rule.

feedback.legit: That expression is an antiderivative, not the derivative. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: That expression is an antiderivative, not the derivative. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: The old exponent becomes the multiplier, then the new exponent is one smaller. That is the pattern I remember.

feedback.legit: The exponent decreases by one and multiplies the result. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: The exponent decreases by one and multiplies the result. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

questions.prompt: My definite integral came out zero even though the function was not zero. Can area below the axis cancel area above it?

answers.text: Yes. A definite integral tracks signed accumulation, so positive and negative contributions can cancel.

feedback.legit: It represents accumulated signed change. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: It represents accumulated signed change. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: No. If the integral is zero, the function had to stay at zero for the entire interval.

feedback.legit: Positive and negative contributions can cancel, so a zero integral does not force the function to vanish. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Positive and negative contributions can cancel, so a zero integral does not force the function to vanish. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Definite integrals are ordinary area, which can never be negative. Cancellation is not part of the definition.

feedback.legit: A definite integral is signed; regions below the axis contribute negatively. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: A definite integral is signed; regions below the axis contribute negatively. That was correctly classified as Sus.

questions.prompt: A student keeps mixing up the chain rule and the product rule. What pattern would you point out first?

answers.text: Differentiate the outside function, leave the inside in place, then multiply by the derivative of that inside function.

feedback.legit: The outer derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner function. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: The outer derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner function. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Look for one function nested inside another. A composition is the chain rule's natural habitat.

feedback.legit: When differentiating a composition of functions. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: When differentiating a composition of functions. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Two functions multiplied together call for the product rule; one function fed into another calls for the chain rule.

feedback.legit: Products use the product rule; compositions use the chain rule. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Products use the product rule; compositions use the chain rule. This one was safe to mark Legit.

questions.prompt: No shortcuts: if `h(x) = f(x)g(x)`, what does `h'(x)` expand to?

answers.text: `h'(x) = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`. Differentiate each factor once while keeping the other one.

feedback.legit: `f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: `f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)`. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Easy: `h'(x) = f'(x)g'(x)`. Just differentiate both sides of the product.

feedback.legit: Differentiating each factor and multiplying omits two required terms. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Differentiating each factor and multiplying omits two required terms. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: If either factor is constant, the derivative of the entire product is zero. Constants kill the product rule.

feedback.legit: A constant factor remains and multiplies the derivative of the other factor. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A constant factor remains and multiplies the derivative of the other factor. Sus was the correct call here.

questions.prompt: How would you explain the Fundamental Theorem without reciting its name? What are differentiation and integration doing to each other?

answers.text: It guarantees an elementary antiderivative for every discontinuous function. That is the theorem's main point.

feedback.legit: The theorem has hypotheses and does not guarantee elementary formulas. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: The theorem has hypotheses and does not guarantee elementary formulas. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Under the right conditions, differentiate an integral whose upper limit moves and you recover the integrand.

feedback.legit: Under suitable conditions, differentiating an integral with a variable upper limit recovers the integrand. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Under suitable conditions, differentiating an integral with a variable upper limit recovers the integrand. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: It ties accumulated change from integration to antiderivatives. In that precise sense, the two operations undo each other.

feedback.legit: It connects accumulation by integration with antiderivatives. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: It connects accumulation by integration with antiderivatives. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

questions.prompt: A curve has no break at a point. Is that enough to know a derivative exists there too?

answers.text: No. `|x|` stays continuous at zero but has a sharp corner there, so the derivative does not exist.

feedback.legit: The absolute-value function is continuous but not differentiable at zero. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: The absolute-value function is continuous but not differentiable at zero. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Yes. If you can draw through the point without lifting your pen, a tangent slope must exist.

feedback.legit: A continuous function can have a corner or cusp and fail to be differentiable. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: A continuous function can have a corner or cusp and fail to be differentiable. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: The implication runs the other way: differentiability at a point guarantees continuity at that point.

feedback.legit: Differentiability implies continuity at that point. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Differentiability implies continuity at that point. This one was safe to mark Legit.

questions.prompt: I found `f'(c) = 0`. Can I call `c` a maximum or minimum already, or is that jumping the gun?

answers.text: Call it an extremum. Every stationary point has to be either a local maximum or a local minimum.

feedback.legit: A stationary point can be neither, as with `x^3` at zero. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A stationary point can be neither, as with `x^3` at zero. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: That only makes `c` a critical point worth investigating. It does not classify the point for you.

feedback.legit: It identifies a critical point that needs further analysis. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: It identifies a critical point that needs further analysis. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Check how the derivative changes sign, or use suitable higher-derivative information, before naming the point.

feedback.legit: Sign changes or higher-derivative information can help classify the point. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Sign changes or higher-derivative information can help classify the point. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

questions.prompt: When I use `u`-substitution in an integral, which differentiation rule am I effectively running backward?

answers.text: It is the quotient rule in reverse, and only the quotient rule. Products and compositions are unrelated.

feedback.legit: Substitution broadly handles compositions paired with an inner derivative. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Substitution broadly handles compositions paired with an inner derivative. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: The bounds of a definite integral stay untouched after substitution. Only the variable name changes.

feedback.legit: For a definite integral, either transform the bounds to the new variable or return to the original variable before evaluating. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: For a definite integral, either transform the bounds to the new variable or return to the original variable before evaluating. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: You are reversing the chain rule: spot an inner expression and account for its derivative.

feedback.legit: The chain rule. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: The chain rule. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: With `∂f/∂x`, what am I pretending stays unchanged while `x` moves?

answers.text: A partial derivative measures the rate of change along one coordinate direction.

feedback.legit: Partial derivatives describe coordinate-direction rates of change. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Partial derivatives describe coordinate-direction rates of change. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Hold the other independent variables fixed, then let `x` vary. That is the whole setup.

feedback.legit: The other independent variables while one variable changes. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: The other independent variables while one variable changes. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: You freeze the function's output value and change `x` underneath it. The other inputs can move freely.

feedback.legit: The function value generally changes as the selected variable changes. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: The function value generally changes as the selected variable changes. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: For a scalar field in Euclidean space, where does the gradient point, and what does its length tell you?

answers.text: When it is nonzero, the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase.

feedback.legit: The direction of steepest local increase when the gradient is nonzero. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: The direction of steepest local increase when the gradient is nonzero. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: It lies tangent to every regular level surface. Following it keeps the scalar value unchanged.

feedback.legit: The gradient is normal to a regular level surface. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: The gradient is normal to a regular level surface. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Its magnitude is the largest directional derivative available among unit directions at that point.

feedback.legit: Its magnitude is the maximum directional derivative over unit directions at that point. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Its magnitude is the maximum directional derivative over unit directions at that point. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: If a function has derivatives of every order, can I safely replace it with its Taylor series everywhere?

answers.text: Absolutely. Every Taylor series converges for every input, so the radius is always infinite.

feedback.legit: A Taylor series can have a finite or even zero radius of convergence. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: A Taylor series can have a finite or even zero radius of convergence. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: More terms always improve the approximation at every input. Taylor error can only decrease.

feedback.legit: Improvement depends on the input, convergence region, and remainder; more terms do not give a universal guarantee. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Improvement depends on the input, convergence region, and remainder; more terms do not give a universal guarantee. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Yes. Infinite differentiability is exactly the condition that makes a function equal its Taylor series.
